title: Tuple

infobox.value: is a finite [sequence](/wiki/Sequence "Sequence") (or ordered list) of [numbers](/wiki/Number "Number"). More generally, it is a sequence of [mathematical objects](/wiki/Mathematical_object "Mathematical object"), called the *elements* of the tuple. An **n-tuple** is a tuple of n elements, where n is a non-negative [integer](/wiki/Integer "Integer"). There is only one 0-tuple, called the *empty tuple*. A 1-tuple and a 2-tuple are commonly called a [*singleton*](/wiki/Singleton_(mathematics) "Singleton (mathematics)") and an *[ordered pair](/wiki/Ordered_pair "Ordered pair")*, respectively. The term *"infinite tuple"* is occasionally used for *"infinite sequences"*.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Finite ordered list of elements

For the musical term, see [Tuplet](/wiki/Tuplet "Tuplet").

"Octuple" redirects here. For the boat, see [Octuple scull](/wiki/Octuple_scull "Octuple scull").

"Sextuple" redirects here. For the sports achievement in association football, see [Sextuple (association football)](/wiki/Sextuple_(association_football) "Sextuple (association football)").

In [mathematics](/wiki/Mathematics "Mathematics"), a **tuple** is a finite [sequence](/wiki/Sequence "Sequence") (or ordered list) of [numbers](/wiki/Number "Number"). More generally, it is a sequence of [mathematical objects](/wiki/Mathematical_object "Mathematical object"), called the *elements* of the tuple. An **n-tuple** is a tuple of n elements, where n is a non-negative [integer](/wiki/Integer "Integer"). There is only one 0-tuple, called the *empty tuple*. A 1-tuple and a 2-tuple are commonly called a [*singleton*](/wiki/Singleton_(mathematics) "Singleton (mathematics)") and an *[ordered pair](/wiki/Ordered_pair "Ordered pair")*, respectively. The term *"infinite tuple"* is occasionally used for *"infinite sequences"*.

Tuples are usually written by listing the elements within parentheses "( )" and separated by commas; for example, (2, 7, 4, 1, 7) denotes a 5-tuple. Other types of brackets are sometimes used, although they may have a different meaning.[[a]](#cite_note-1)

An n-tuple can be formally defined as the [image](/wiki/Image_(mathematics) "Image (mathematics)") of a [function](/wiki/Function_(mathematics) "Function (mathematics)") that has the set of the first n [natural numbers](/wiki/Natural_number "Natural number") as its [domain](/wiki/Domain_of_a_function "Domain of a function") (1, 2, ..., *n*). Tuples may be also defined from ordered pairs by a [recurrence](/wiki/Recurrence_relation "Recurrence relation") starting from an ordered pair; indeed, an n-tuple can be identified with the ordered pair of its (*n* − 1) first elements and its nth element, for example, ![{\displaystyle \left(\left(\left(1,2\right),3\right),4\right)=\left(1,2,3,4\right)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/6f6d62a8b2f73f7b906f1d94fb72f68b4a4b76f6).

In [computer science](/wiki/Computer_science "Computer science"), tuples come in many forms. Most typed [functional programming](/wiki/Functional_programming "Functional programming") languages implement tuples directly as [product types](/wiki/Product_type "Product type"),[[1]](#cite_note-2) tightly associated with [algebraic data types](/wiki/Algebraic_data_type "Algebraic data type"), [pattern matching](/wiki/Pattern_matching "Pattern matching"), and [destructuring assignment](/wiki/Assignment_(computer_science)#Parallel_assignment "Assignment (computer science)").[[2]](#cite_note-3) Many programming languages offer an alternative to tuples, known as [record types](/wiki/Record_(computer_science) "Record (computer science)"), featuring unordered elements accessed by label.[[3]](#cite_note-4) A few programming languages combine ordered tuple product types and unordered record types into a single construct, as in [C structs](/wiki/Struct_(C_programming_language) "Struct (C programming language)") and Haskell records. [Relational databases](/wiki/Relational_database "Relational database") may formally identify their [rows](/wiki/Row_(database) "Row (database)") (records) as *tuples*.

Tuples also occur in [relational algebra](/wiki/Relational_algebra "Relational algebra"); when programming the [semantic web](/wiki/Semantic_web "Semantic web") with the [Resource Description Framework](/wiki/Resource_Description_Framework "Resource Description Framework") (RDF); in [linguistics](/wiki/Linguistics "Linguistics");[[4]](#cite_note-5) and in [philosophy](/wiki/Philosophy "Philosophy").[[5]](#cite_note-6)

## Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tuple&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology")]

The term originated as an abstraction of the sequence: single, couple/double, triple, quadruple, quintuple, sextuple, septuple, octuple, ..., *n*‑tuple, ..., where the prefixes are taken from the [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") names of the numerals. The unique 0-tuple is called the *null tuple* or *empty tuple*. A 1‑tuple is called a *single* (or *singleton*), a 2‑tuple is called an *ordered pair* or *couple*, and a 3‑tuple is called a *triple* (or *triplet*). The number *n* can be any nonnegative [integer](/wiki/Integer "Integer"). For example, a [complex number](/wiki/Complex_number "Complex number") can be represented as a 2‑tuple of reals, a [quaternion](/wiki/Quaternion "Quaternion") can be represented as a 4‑tuple, an [octonion](/wiki/Octonion "Octonion") can be represented as an 8‑tuple, and a [sedenion](/wiki/Sedenion "Sedenion") can be represented as a 16‑tuple.

Although these uses treat *‑tuple* as the suffix, the original suffix was *‑ple* as in "triple" (three-fold) or "decuple" (ten‑fold). This originates from [medieval Latin](/wiki/Medieval_Latin "Medieval Latin") *plus* (meaning "more") related to [Greek](/wiki/Greek_language "Greek language") ‑πλοῦς, which replaced the classical and late antique *‑plex* (meaning "folded"), as in "duplex".[[6]](#cite_note-7)[[b]](#cite_note-8)

## Properties

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tuple&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Properties")]

The general rule for the identity of two *n*-tuples is

:   ![{\displaystyle (a_{1},a_{2},\ldots ,a_{n})=(b_{1},b_{2},\ldots ,b_{n})}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/49be7e45f8d23903e98eca710eb3b60027ec99ac) [if and only if](/wiki/If_and_only_if "If and only if") ![{\displaystyle a_{1}=b_{1},{\text{ }}a_{2}=b_{2},{\text{ }}\ldots ,{\text{ }}a_{n}=b_{n}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/c97fec0789335a54504f52c801916baa7c7c8cff).

Thus a tuple has properties that distinguish it from a [set](/wiki/Set_(mathematics) "Set (mathematics)"):

1. A tuple may contain multiple instances of the same element, so   
   tuple ![{\displaystyle (1,2,2,3)\neq (1,2,3)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/945a47fa01742e04ba4fc4cbd931dfd5b17e8c91); but set ![{\displaystyle \{1,2,2,3\}=\{1,2,3\}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/f04a7126a817675476ef684c59e9f83a0b414a1f).
2. Tuple elements are ordered: tuple ![{\displaystyle (1,2,3)\neq (3,2,1)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/eb286d33ffedcfeda837cf023d49a2183f18f495), but set ![{\displaystyle \{1,2,3\}=\{3,2,1\}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/04feef21743d18c28161ceb0349fd4b9c2fac481).
3. A tuple has a finite number of elements, while a set or a [multiset](/wiki/Multiset "Multiset") may have an infinite number of elements.

## Definitions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tuple&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Definitions")]

There are several definitions of tuples that give them the properties described in the previous section.

### Tuples as functions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tuple&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Tuples as functions")]

The ![{\displaystyle 0}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/2aae8864a3c1fec9585261791a809ddec1489950)-tuple may be identified as the [empty function](/wiki/Function_(mathematics)#General_properties "Function (mathematics)"). For ![{\displaystyle n\geq 1,}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/cc38ec6af7dd11fdc9baa67365f23906d76da4bb) the ![{\displaystyle n}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/a601995d55609f2d9f5e233e36fbe9ea26011b3b)-tuple ![{\displaystyle \left(a_{1},\ldots ,a_{n}\right)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/5db29ad6ba003589bd041c0fa6a80fb2cadb2387) may be identified with the [surjective](/wiki/Surjective_function "Surjective function") [function](/wiki/Function_(mathematics)#Definition "Function (mathematics)")

:   ![{\displaystyle F~:~\left\{1,\ldots ,n\right\}~\to ~\left\{a_{1},\ldots ,a_{n}\right\}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/129f0967b4c42d8fa9266b46024c755da76f976e)

with [domain](/wiki/Domain_of_a_function "Domain of a function")

:   ![{\displaystyle \operatorname {domain} F=\left\{1,\ldots ,n\right\}=\left\{i\in \mathbb {N} :1\leq i\leq n\right\}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/b98f30c83cb9fe20f16229c4e5f86effa9c1ccd1)

and with [codomain](/wiki/Codomain "Codomain")

:   ![{\displaystyle \operatorname {codomain} F=\left\{a_{1},\ldots ,a_{n}\right\},}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/d3dfce7d26890cc8aa011fc1989839c36223212f)

that is defined at ![{\displaystyle i\in \operatorname {domain} F=\left\{1,\ldots ,n\right\}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/e9bd109dbfd09785efd0903d00bd313d80f9c1ab) by

:   ![{\displaystyle F(i):=a_{i}.}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/1848c5f51ce74c696849267565d08da0b0719e01)

That is, ![{\displaystyle F}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/545fd099af8541605f7ee55f08225526be88ce57) is the function defined by

:   ![{\displaystyle {\begin{alignedat}{3}1\;&\mapsto &&\;a_{1}\\\;&\;\;\vdots &&\;\\n\;&\mapsto &&\;a_{n}\\\end{alignedat}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/89ca068d1878ca2324993ec8e67dffe446b10b32)

in which case the equality

:   ![{\displaystyle \left(a_{1},a_{2},\dots ,a_{n}\right)=\left(F(1),F(2),\dots ,F(n)\right)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/173d649b045785edb41db113179e16251477ace6)

necessarily holds.

Tuples as sets of ordered pairs

Functions are commonly identified with their [graphs](/wiki/Graph_of_a_function "Graph of a function"), which is a certain set of ordered pairs.
Indeed, many authors use graphs as the definition of a function.
Using this definition of "function", the above function ![{\displaystyle F}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/545fd099af8541605f7ee55f08225526be88ce57) can be defined as:

:   ![{\displaystyle F~:=~\left\{\left(1,a_{1}\right),\ldots ,\left(n,a_{n}\right)\right\}.}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/76baa6bf73b88fbc2950c9e6fb48f73be0353645)

### Tuples as nested ordered pairs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tuple&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Tuples as nested ordered pairs")]

Another way of modeling tuples in set theory is as nested [ordered pairs](/wiki/Ordered_pair "Ordered pair"). This approach assumes that the notion of ordered pair has already been defined.

1. The 0-tuple (i.e. the empty tuple) is represented by the empty set ![{\displaystyle \emptyset }](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/6af50205f42bb2ec3c666b7b847d2c7f96e464c7).
2. An *n*-tuple, with *n* > 0, can be defined as an ordered pair of its first entry and an (*n* − 1)-tuple (which contains the remaining entries when *n* > 1):

   :   ![{\displaystyle (a_{1},a_{2},a_{3},\ldots ,a_{n})=(a_{1},(a_{2},a_{3},\ldots ,a_{n}))}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/2698402ce9489cc8059e92ff8e5dbf8a1589b301)

This definition can be applied recursively to the (*n* − 1)-tuple:

:   ![{\displaystyle (a_{1},a_{2},a_{3},\ldots ,a_{n})=(a_{1},(a_{2},(a_{3},(\ldots ,(a_{n},\emptyset )\ldots ))))}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/86d7088e31e634c43425cfbdc23d19e20bd4b699)

Thus, for example:

:   ![{\displaystyle {\begin{aligned}(1,2,3)&=(1,(2,(3,\emptyset )))\\(1,2,3,4)&=(1,(2,(3,(4,\emptyset ))))\\\end{aligned}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/3656e1d003f033bc8a46dad6198f35cf84dadb3a)

A variant of this definition starts "peeling off" elements from the other end:

1. The 0-tuple is the empty set ![{\displaystyle \emptyset }](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/6af50205f42bb2ec3c666b7b847d2c7f96e464c7).
2. For *n* > 0:

   :   ![{\displaystyle (a_{1},a_{2},a_{3},\ldots ,a_{n})=((a_{1},a_{2},a_{3},\ldots ,a_{n-1}),a_{n})}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/6e930380928a844376aa6bf8737dbb65b5a789ec)

This definition can be applied recursively:

:   ![{\displaystyle (a_{1},a_{2},a_{3},\ldots ,a_{n})=((\ldots (((\emptyset ,a_{1}),a_{2}),a_{3}),\ldots ),a_{n})}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/6b973e7b4db6ce38786bf2affa33fbdccfa66410)

Thus, for example:

:   ![{\displaystyle {\begin{aligned}(1,2,3)&=(((\emptyset ,1),2),3)\\(1,2,3,4)&=((((\emptyset ,1),2),3),4)\\\end{aligned}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/118f0960978983f2adab89a72c1b80d7774787ea)

### Tuples as nested sets

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tuple&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Tuples as nested sets")]

Using [Kuratowski's representation for an ordered pair](/wiki/Ordered_pair#Kuratowski's_definition "Ordered pair"), the second definition above can be reformulated in terms of pure [set theory](/wiki/Set_theory "Set theory"):

1. The 0-tuple (i.e. the empty tuple) is represented by the empty set ![{\displaystyle \emptyset }](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/6af50205f42bb2ec3c666b7b847d2c7f96e464c7);
2. Let ![{\displaystyle x}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/87f9e315fd7e2ba406057a97300593c4802b53e4) be an *n*-tuple ![{\displaystyle (a_{1},a_{2},\ldots ,a_{n})}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/ce7edfb2f544f6e6158189ea1af9d07141fc996b), and let ![{\displaystyle x\rightarrow b\equiv (a_{1},a_{2},\ldots ,a_{n},b)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/a2c0aac90de0b68b17eefbb3a5d191d1b4fbf757). Then, ![{\displaystyle x\rightarrow b\equiv \{\{x\},\{x,b\}\}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/3762e24a28653c7e366fbed8be0937f4338bac3f). (The right arrow, ![{\displaystyle \rightarrow }](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/53e574cc3aa5b4bf5f3f5906caf121a378eef08b), could be read as "adjoined with".)

In this formulation:

:   ![{\displaystyle {\begin{array}{lclcl}()&&&=&\emptyset \\&&&&\\(1)&=&()\rightarrow 1&=&\{\{()\},\{(),1\}\}\\&&&=&\{\{\emptyset \},\{\emptyset ,1\}\}\\&&&&\\(1,2)&=&(1)\rightarrow 2&=&\{\{(1)\},\{(1),2\}\}\\&&&=&\{\{\{\{\emptyset \},\{\emptyset ,1\}\}\},\\&&&&\{\{\{\emptyset \},\{\emptyset ,1\}\},2\}\}\\&&&&\\(1,2,3)&=&(1,2)\rightarrow 3&=&\{\{(1,2)\},\{(1,2),3\}\}\\&&&=&\{\{\{\{\{\{\emptyset \},\{\emptyset ,1\}\}\},\\&&&&\{\{\{\emptyset \},\{\emptyset ,1\}\},2\}\}\},\\&&&&\{\{\{\{\{\emptyset \},\{\emptyset ,1\}\}\},\\&&&&\{\{\{\emptyset \},\{\emptyset ,1\}\},2\}\},3\}\}\\\end{array}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/5654421f5c61a66d3dd75921c42fb9de304b727b)

## *n*-tuples of *m*-sets

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tuple&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: n-tuples of m-sets")]

In [discrete mathematics](/wiki/Discrete_mathematics "Discrete mathematics"), especially [combinatorics](/wiki/Combinatorics "Combinatorics") and finite [probability theory](/wiki/Probability_theory "Probability theory"), *n*-tuples arise in the context of various counting problems and are treated more informally as ordered lists of length *n*.[[7]](#cite_note-9) *n*-tuples whose entries come from a set of *m* elements are also called *arrangements with repetition*, *[permutations of a multiset](/wiki/Permutation#Permutations_of_multisets "Permutation")* and, in some non-English literature, *variations with repetition*. The number of *n*-tuples of an *m*-set is *m**n*. This follows from the combinatorial [rule of product](/wiki/Rule_of_product "Rule of product").[[8]](#cite_note-10) If *S* is a finite set of [cardinality](/wiki/Cardinality "Cardinality") *m*, this number is the cardinality of the *n*-fold [Cartesian power](/wiki/Cartesian_product#n-ary_Cartesian_power "Cartesian product") *S* × *S* × ⋯ × *S*. Tuples are elements of this product set.

## Type theory

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tuple&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Type theory")]

Main article: [Product type](/wiki/Product_type "Product type")

In [type theory](/wiki/Type_theory "Type theory"), commonly used in [programming languages](/wiki/Programming_language "Programming language"), a tuple has a [product type](/wiki/Product_type "Product type"); this fixes not only the length, but also the underlying types of each component. Formally:

:   ![{\displaystyle (x_{1},x_{2},\ldots ,x_{n}):{\mathsf {T}}_{1}\times {\mathsf {T}}_{2}\times \ldots \times {\mathsf {T}}_{n}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/d1b990b0ddf99b248978055b4a78e7785063fc04)

and the [projections](/wiki/Projection_(mathematics) "Projection (mathematics)") are term constructors:

:   ![{\displaystyle \pi _{1}(x):{\mathsf {T}}_{1},~\pi _{2}(x):{\mathsf {T}}_{2},~\ldots ,~\pi _{n}(x):{\mathsf {T}}_{n}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/3672cdb5ab9186a72830406d60c8c4ddc85ba40e)

The tuple with labeled elements used in the [relational model](/wiki/Relational_model "Relational model") has a [record type](/wiki/Record_(computer_science) "Record (computer science)"). Both of these types can be defined as simple extensions of the [simply typed lambda calculus](/wiki/Simply_typed_lambda_calculus "Simply typed lambda calculus").[[9]](#cite_note-pierce2002-11)

The notion of a tuple in type theory and that in set theory are related in the following way: If we consider the natural [model](/wiki/Model_theory "Model theory") of a type theory, and use the Scott brackets to indicate the semantic interpretation, then the model consists of some sets ![{\displaystyle S_{1},S_{2},\ldots ,S_{n}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/ea5ce7e68d6705e88490d004da9231c1187a278a) (note: the use of italics here that distinguishes sets from types) such that:

:   ![{\displaystyle [\![{\mathsf {T}}_{1}]\!]=S_{1},~[\![{\mathsf {T}}_{2}]\!]=S_{2},~\ldots ,~[\![{\mathsf {T}}_{n}]\!]=S_{n}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/3228b107459805fcc4e392aa44ebf4cf09d11e2e)

and the interpretation of the basic terms is:

:   ![{\displaystyle [\![x_{1}]\!]\in [\![{\mathsf {T}}_{1}]\!],~[\![x_{2}]\!]\in [\![{\mathsf {T}}_{2}]\!],~\ldots ,~[\![x_{n}]\!]\in [\![{\mathsf {T}}_{n}]\!]}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/d9cf377e82ba4900f83ef458333da98cd1a90f45).

The *n*-tuple of type theory has the natural interpretation as an *n*-tuple of set theory:[[10]](#cite_note-12)

:   ![{\displaystyle [\![(x_{1},x_{2},\ldots ,x_{n})]\!]=(\,[\![x_{1}]\!],[\![x_{2}]\!],\ldots ,[\![x_{n}]\!]\,)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/c317c37e9e0ecfa60a90cddc0c319cef6ae63fea)

The [unit type](/wiki/Unit_type "Unit type") has as semantic interpretation the 0-tuple.

For a list of tuple types in programming languages, see [Product type#Product types in programming languages](/wiki/Product_type#Product_types_in_programming_languages "Product type").

## Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tuple&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Notes")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-1)** [Square brackets](/wiki/Square_bracket "Square bracket") are used for [matrices](/wiki/Matrix_(mathematics) "Matrix (mathematics)"), including [row vectors](/wiki/Row_vector "Row vector"). [Braces](/wiki/Braces_(punctuation) "Braces (punctuation)") are used for [sets](/wiki/Set_(mathematics) "Set (mathematics)"). Each [programming language](/wiki/Programming_language "Programming language") has its own convention for the different brackets.
2. **[^](#cite_ref-8)** Compare the etymology of [ploidy](/wiki/Ploidy "Ploidy"), from the Greek for -fold.
